q: A logistics agent has two tools: `cancel_shipment` (voids a not-yet-dispatched shipment) and `recall_shipment` (issues a return-to-sender on an in-transit shipment). Dashboards show the model picks `cancel_shipment` for in-transit packages 38% of the time, causing failed cancellations. Both descriptions read only 'Stops a shipment.' What is the most effective FIRST step?

options.A: Expand each description to state the shipment state it applies to (pre-dispatch vs in-transit) and a when-not-to-use boundary line
options.B: Merge the two tools into one `stop_shipment` that inspects the shipment state internally and branches to the correct void or recall path
options.C: Add a five-example few-shot block to the system prompt contrasting cancel-vs-recall cases the model can pattern-match against
options.D: Insert an LLM routing classifier ahead of the toolset that pre-decides cancel vs recall before either tool is ever exposed

explanation: Tool descriptions are the primary signal the model uses to select tools, and these are nearly empty, so expanding them with the distinguishing shipment state plus a when-not-to-use boundary is the low-effort, high-leverage first fix; extra justification belongs here, not crammed into the option. B (merging) discards a meaningful least-privilege boundary and is over-engineered as a first move. C (few-shot) adds tokens and is more brittle than fixing the descriptions the model actually reads at selection time. D (routing classifier) bolts on a whole component and latency to compensate for descriptions that were never written properly.

q: An MCP server exposes `apply_promo_code` with input schema `{"code": {"type":"string"}}` and no other constraints. Production logs show calls with `code: ""`, `code: "  SAVE10 "`, and `code: null` slipping through; the handler then matches no promo and returns `{"status":"ok","discount":0}`. Which change best addresses the root problem?

options.A: Tighten the schema (minLength, pattern, required) and return a structured `{"error":"promo_not_found"}` instead of a zero-discount success
options.B: Leave the schema as-is but emit a warning log on every zero-discount result so analysts can review malformed codes after the fact
options.C: Trim and uppercase the code inside the handler before lookup, then still return `{"status":"ok","discount":0}` whenever nothing matches
options.D: Change the return to a bare `{"status":"ok"}` and drop the discount field entirely so the model stops over-trusting a zero value

explanation: Two defects compound: a loose schema lets malformed input through, and the tool silently succeeds on failure. Tightening the schema rejects bad input at the validation layer and a structured error makes the failure machine-readable so the agent can react. B only adds passive logging and never stops the silent success. C fixes input hygiene but still reports success when the promo does not exist, masking the failure. D removes information and still signals 'ok' on a failed lookup, the silent-success anti-pattern.

q: A team is debating what the Model Context Protocol (MCP) actually standardizes before adopting it across three internal agents. Which statement is correct?

options.A: MCP standardizes how an application interoperably exposes tools, resources, and prompts so clients discover and call them without per-integration glue
options.B: MCP is a quantized weight-exchange format that lets one model's trained tool-use head be loaded and reused by a different model
options.C: MCP is a payload compression scheme that shrinks large tool-call arguments and results on the wire to cut token and bandwidth cost
options.D: MCP is a replacement for HTTPS that supplies its own transport-layer encryption and certificate handling between client and server

explanation: MCP is an open protocol standardizing the interoperable exposure of tools, resources, and prompts so any compliant client can discover and invoke them. B is wrong: MCP has nothing to do with model weights or quantization. C is wrong: it defines message structure, not byte-level compression. D is wrong: MCP is not a security/transport encryption layer and does not replace HTTPS; it can run over transports like stdio or HTTP.

q: You are modeling a customer-support agent's access to a knowledge base of help articles and to a ticket system. Articles are queried frequently for grounding and are never modified by the agent; tickets are created and updated by the agent. In MCP terms, how should these be modeled?

options.A: Expose the help articles as a read-only resource the agent only reads for grounding, and model ticket creation and update as separate side-effecting tools, each with an explicit input schema validating the required fields
options.B: Expose both the articles and the ticket actions as tools, since anything the agent reaches for at runtime is fundamentally a callable tool
options.C: Expose the ticket actions as read-only resources and the articles as tools, since articles turn over far more often than open tickets do
options.D: Expose both the articles and the tickets as resources, since the agent only needs to read article text and read current ticket state back

explanation: MCP resources are read-only contextual data, fitting help articles the agent only reads for grounding; tools are callable, side-effecting actions with input schemas, fitting ticket create/update. B over-models the read-only articles as tools, losing the resource/tool distinction. C inverts the roles entirely. D ignores that the agent must create and update tickets, which are side-effecting actions, not read-only context.

q: A DevOps agent has a single tool `manage_infra` whose description says 'Manage infrastructure: create, resize, reboot, snapshot, or terminate any VM, plus edit firewall rules and DNS.' Mis-selection is low, but a malformed call once terminated a production VM the agent only meant to reboot, and security flags that any task grants the model termination power. What is the best redesign?

options.A: Split it into narrowly scoped tools (reboot_vm, resize_vm, terminate_vm) each with a schema, and confirm the destructive ones
options.B: Keep the single tool but add a required `confirm: true` field that must be set on every call, regardless of which sub-action is being invoked
options.C: Keep `manage_infra` and write a much longer description that enumerates every sub-action, its arguments, and its blast radius and risks
options.D: Keep the single tool but rename it `safe_manage_infra` so the descriptor itself signals caution and nudges the model toward careful use

explanation: A 'do everything' tool violates least privilege and bundles a destructive action (terminate) with benign ones, so every task implicitly grants termination. Splitting into scoped tools lets you grant only what a task needs and confirm specifically the destructive operations. B blanket-confirms even harmless reboots, training model and users to rubber-stamp confirmations. C lengthens the description but still exposes full termination power on every call. D renames without changing the actual privilege surface, which is purely cosmetic.

q: An agent calls a `charge_card` tool. Because of a network timeout the client retries the same call three times with identical arguments, and metrics show some customers were charged twice. The tool currently accepts `{amount, currency, customer_id}` and processes every call. What design change most directly prevents the double charges?

options.A: Require a caller-supplied `idempotency_key` on every charge so the server records the key, treats any retry carrying the same key as the one original operation, and returns the original result instead of charging again
options.B: Lower the client's automatic retry count from three to one so the tool is simply invoked fewer times under transient network errors
options.C: Wrap each charge in a try/catch, detect duplicates server-side, and return `{"status":"ok"}` for the duplicate so the agent keeps moving
options.D: Add a human confirmation step before every single charge so an operator can review the amount and catch any duplicate request manually before money actually moves

explanation: Idempotency is the correct property for a destructive financial action: an idempotency key lets the server recognize a retried request as the same operation and avoid charging twice while still returning a coherent result. B reduces but does not eliminate the race and abandons resilience to transient failures. C hides duplicate detection behind a generic success, a silent-success anti-pattern that may already have double-charged. D adds human friction to every charge and does not make the operation safe under automated retries.

q: A reporting agent exposes `run_query` with input `{"sql": {"type":"string"}}` and the description 'Run a query.' Telemetry shows the model frequently passes natural-language strings ('top customers last month') instead of SQL, and also occasionally passes DROP/DELETE statements. Which combination of fixes is the soundest first step?

options.A: State in the description that it expects a read-only SELECT, with an example, and have the handler reject non-SELECT input
options.B: Add a second tool `run_natural_language_query` alongside the SQL one and route between them with a classifier that inspects each incoming request
options.C: Silently parse any natural-language input into the equivalent SQL inside the handler and execute the generated statement without surfacing it
options.D: Keep the current schema unchanged and attach a post-hoc audit log that records every statement executed so misuse can be reviewed afterward

explanation: The description fails to specify the required input format (SQL, read-only), which drives the model to send natural language, and there is no validation against destructive statements. Clarifying the expected format with an example plus rejecting non-SELECT input with a structured error fixes both at the right layer. B introduces a routing classifier and a new tool, over-engineered as a first move. C silently transforms input, hiding behavior and risking incorrect queries run without the agent's awareness. D only records damage after the fact and prevents nothing.

q: While drafting an MCP server, a developer must choose between stdio and HTTP transport. The server runs as a local subprocess spawned by the desktop client on the same machine, with no remote callers. Conceptually, which choice and rationale is most appropriate?

options.A: stdio, because the desktop client launches the server as a local child process on the same machine and exchanges JSON-RPC messages by writing requests to its standard input and reading responses from its standard output, needing no network endpoint
options.B: HTTP only, because MCP messages can only be framed as HTTP request bodies and stdio has no way to carry structured tool-call payloads
options.C: stdio, because the stdio transport encrypts the channel end-to-end whereas HTTP would transmit the tool arguments in readable plaintext
options.D: HTTP, because a spawned local subprocess has no other channel and must open a loopback TCP port before it can exchange any MCP messages

explanation: For a locally spawned subprocess, stdio is the natural transport: the client writes requests to the server's stdin and reads responses from its stdout, with no network endpoint required. B is false; MCP supports multiple transports including stdio, which carries structured messages fine. C invents an encryption property; stdio is chosen for local process communication, not encryption. D is false; a subprocess communicates over its standard streams and need not open a TCP port.

q: An agent has `send_email` and `create_draft_email`. The model picks `send_email` when the user says 'write up an email to legal so I can review it' 27% of the time, immediately dispatching unreviewed mail. Descriptions are 'Send an email.' and 'Make a draft email.' Beyond improving descriptions, which additional safeguard best fits the irreversibility of sending?

options.A: Treat `send_email` as irreversible and require a confirmation step before dispatch, while leaving the draft tool unconfirmed
options.B: Require an identical confirmation step on both tools equally so the model treats sending and drafting as interchangeable, well-guarded options
options.C: Remove `create_draft_email` entirely and always send, on the assumption that users can recall or delete a sent message afterward if needed
options.D: Have `send_email` return `{"status":"ok"}` whether or not the recipient address resolves, so a bad address never stalls the agent mid-task

explanation: Sending mail is effectively irreversible, so a confirmation gate on the destructive action is the right safeguard, while the reversible draft action should stay friction-free to preserve usability. B confirms a harmless draft equally, blurring the boundary and encouraging reflexive approval. C removes the safe alternative the user actually asked for and wrongly assumes recall is reliable. D introduces a silent-success failure mode unrelated to the confirmation concern and is itself an anti-pattern.

q: A weather-aware travel agent exposes `get_forecast`. Its description reads 'Returns the weather as a friendly summary string like "Looks rainy, bring an umbrella!"' Downstream code needs to branch on precipitation probability and temperature, and parsing the prose fails ~12% of the time. What is the best correction?

options.A: Return a machine-readable result (e.g. `{"temp_c":14,"precip_prob":0.7,"condition":"rain"}`) and document the schema in the description
options.B: Keep returning the prose string but instruct the model to re-read it more carefully and extract the numbers before it branches on them
options.C: Append the structured fields to the end of the existing prose sentence so both the friendly summary and the parseable data live in one string
options.D: Keep the prose format but localize the summary to the user's own language, on the theory that native-language text parses more reliably

explanation: Tools consumed programmatically should return machine-readable results, so emitting structured fields the caller can branch on directly eliminates the fragile prose parsing. B relies on best-effort string interpretation, the exact unreliable path causing the 12% failures. C still forces parsing of free text and mixes formats, compounding fragility. D changes the prose language but leaves it unstructured, making parsing no more reliable and arguably worse.

q: A finance agent exposes `delete_invoice` whose schema is `{"invoice_id": {"type":"string"}}`. The description says only 'Deletes an invoice.' Audit logs show the model occasionally calls it with `invoice_id` values it guessed or partially constructed, deleting the wrong records, and there is no way to undo. Which set of changes best aligns with safe tool design?

options.A: Require a well-formed `invoice_id` pattern in the schema, document that ids must come from a prior lookup rather than being guessed or constructed, and gate this irreversible delete behind an explicit confirmation step
options.B: Make the delete idempotent so that repeating the same id is harmless, then consider the wrong-record problem fully solved by that property alone
options.C: Broaden the tool into `manage_invoice` covering create, read, and delete in one place, on the theory that more context makes the model guess less
options.D: Return `{"status":"ok"}` on every delete call, including ones where no invoice matched the id, so a missed match never stalls the running agent

explanation: The failure stems from fabricated identifiers feeding an irreversible action. Constraining the id format at validation, instructing that ids must originate from a real lookup rather than being invented, and gating the destructive call behind confirmation together address input quality and irreversibility. B addresses repeat-safety but does nothing about deleting the wrong record the first time. C creates an over-broad 'do everything' tool that widens the privilege surface. D is a silent-success anti-pattern that hides failed or wrong deletes behind a generic 'ok'.
